explanation: Crowds of upset or angry people
a schoolyard fight. See also AMBIENCE-PROTEST.

discriminators: Loud, wide, voiced crowd bed with strained phonation and chanting bursts. Acoustically an angry crowd and a jubilant one are near-identical energetic voiced beds — the affect is semantic, not spectral.
Louder and rougher than CROWDS-WALLA, but the boundary against CROWDS-CHEERING and CROWDS-BATTLE is carried by text, not signal.
Voiced, so it is cleanly separable from CROWDS-APPLAUSE.

explanation: Crowds where the applause is predominantly featured. Use CROWDS-CHEERING for recordings that are mainly vocals.

discriminators: The flagship voicing test. UCS: 'crowds where the applause is predominantly featured' vs CROWDS-CHEERING for 'recordings that are mainly vocals'. Applause is a DENSE STOCHASTIC IMPULSE TRAIN of hand claps: voicing_ratio near ZERO, no f0, broadband and bright.
High onset_rate (tens per second) with LOW onset_periodicity — many clappers, uncorrelated, so it is irregular, not rhythmic. A synchronized slow hand-clap is the exception that raises periodicity.
Against CROWDS-CHEERING the difference is stark and reliable: cheering is voiced with mid-band vocal energy; applause has neither.
Against RAIN or FIRE (the same texture morphology) the separation is semantic, not signal — all are dense stochastic impulse trains.
Massed shouting, screaming and dying: a wide, loud, rough-phonation voiced scene with high flux and low stationarity. Acoustically a battle crowd and a panicking crowd are the same object.
UCS rule: if gunfire or explosions are included it is AMBIENCE-WARFARE, not this — so a heavy impulsive/burst component should route away from CROWDS entirely.
The affect boundary against CROWDS-ANGRY and CROWDS-PANIC is text-only.
A mixed party scene: voiced revelry, laughter, some clapping and general chatter. UCS explicitly notes the overlap with CROWDS-CHEERING, and sends the non-clean version to AMBIENCE-CELEBRATION.
There is no acoustic feature that says 'holiday' — the occasion is semantic. Signal can only rule out non-crowds.
More heterogeneous than CROWDS-CHEERING (a sustained vocal swell) and more energetic than CROWDS-WALLA.

explanation: but cheering crowds where the crowd vocals are predominant. Overlap with CROWDS-CELEBRATION.

discriminators: The other half of the flagship voicing test. UCS: 'cheering crowds where the crowd vocals are predominant' — it 'could include applause', but the VOICE must dominate. So voicing_ratio is high (>0.5) and the energy sits in the 200 Hz–2 kHz vocal band.
Against CROWDS-APPLAUSE this is the sharpest contrast in the category: applause is voicing_ratio ≈ 0, bright, broadband and impulsive; cheering is voicing_ratio ≈ 0.7, mid-band and sustained.
Wide, multi-talker and smeared, unlike the single-f0 VOICES-CHEER, which UCS reserves for one person.

explanation: Relatively clean vocals of children together

discriminators: A crowd bed whose f0 population and spectral centroid sit far higher than an adult crowd — children's voices push the whole long-term average spectrum up, and the sub-200 Hz share nearly vanishes.
That brightness is a real, measurable offset against CROWDS-WALLA and CROWDS-CONVERSATION, but it overlaps a high-energy adult crowd; narrowable, not separable.
Shrieks of play blur into CROWDS-PANIC on level and roughness; the sustained playful bed is what tips it here.

explanation: A small enough group chat that you can discern words
smaller and less active than a walla. A chatter.

discriminators: UCS rule: 'a small enough group that you can discern words, smaller and less active than a walla'. Few talkers means the syllabic structure SURVIVES — the 3–8 Hz modulation stays sharp and the energy envelope is eventful (low stationarity, turn-taking gaps).
CROWDS-WALLA is the opposite: enough overlapping talkers that the syllabic peaks average out into a stationary bed.
Louder and more articulate than CROWDS-QUIET, which is murmuring at a very low level.

Many voiced laugh-pulse trains superimposed: still strongly voiced, but the crisp ~4–6 /s periodicity of VOICES-LAUGH is washed out by uncorrelated laughers, so onset_rate rises while onset_periodicity falls.
Voiced, which cleanly separates it from CROWDS-APPLAUSE despite both being bursty.
Typically a swell — fast rise then decay over a few seconds — unlike the sustained bed of CROWDS-WALLA.

explanation: usually performed for or to picture
specific callouts. See also ARCHIVED-LOOP GROUP for official loop group recordings.

discriminators: Defined by HOW it was made — 'usually performed for or to picture' by a cast of actors — not by how it sounds. A loop-group walla and a real walla are the same object to DSP. Excluded from signal scoring; text must drive.
The only weak correlates are studio provenance: drier, narrower and with a lower noise floor than a field-captured CROWDS-WALLA. That is a hint, not a decision.
See also ARCHIVED-LOOP GROUP for official loop group recordings.

explanation: Crowd sounds not fitting another category in this list. See also MOVEMENT-CROWD.

discriminators: The category's abstain target: multiple humans, but no sibling morphology (applause, cheering, laughter, walla, singing) wins clearly. See also MOVEMENT-CROWD for the non-vocal crowd-motion case.

explanation: mixed screams and crying and reactions… crowds in a natural disaster or crowds in a giant monster attack...

discriminators: The only crowd whose voicing rides ROUGH, HIGH-f0 phonation en masse — screams and cries push the crowd's pitch population past 350 Hz with high inharmonicity, and the scene churns (low stationarity, high flux).

That distress phonation is a real measurable offset from CROWDS-CHEERING, which is voiced at ordinary pitch and much steadier — but a euphoric stadium roar can look similar, so it narrows rather than decides.
Overlaps CROWDS-BATTLE almost completely on signal; the difference is context and is carried by text.

explanation: mostly mumbling or murmuring. Like crowds whispering in church or a courtroom scene. See also VOICES-WHISPER for single person recordings.

discriminators: Mumbling and murmuring: a voiced crowd bed at a very LOW absolute level, dark (little energy above 2 kHz — no projected voice, no consonant splash) and very stationary.

The split against CROWDS-WALLA is LEVEL and BRIGHTNESS, not kind: both are stationary voiced beds of indiscernible words. That makes it narrowable, and level alone is fragile against gain staging — require text agreement.
The single-person equivalent per UCS is VOICES-WHISPER, but that one is UNVOICED; a murmuring crowd still carries voicing, so the two are not the same test.

explanation: big group responses to events happening. Shocked reactions.

discriminators: A collective single-syllable swell — a big 'ooo', 'aaah' or 'boo'. Strongly voiced but with LOW 3–8 Hz syllabic modulation, because the whole crowd holds one vowel; that pairing is the discriminator against CROWDS-WALLA and CROWDS-CONVERSATION, which are voiced AND syllabic.
One shaped event (rise, hold, decay) rather than a sustained bed — short, with a mid envelope centroid.
Voiced, so it separates from CROWDS-APPLAUSE; it is the group version of VOICES-REACTION, so it is wide and multi-f0 where that one is narrow and single-f0.

explanation: New Year's Eve crowds singing Auld Lang Syne
Christmas carols. Not large choral music
which would go in MUSICAL category.

discriminators: A crowd holding pitch in unison: voicing and harmonicity go high, sustain_ratio goes high, and the syllabic modulation DROPS because notes are held — plus a metrical onset periodicity (a tempo) that no other crowd has.
That tonal-plus-metrical combination separates it from CROWDS-CHEERING and CROWDS-WALLA, which are aperiodic and far less harmonic.
UCS rule: large choral music belongs in MUSICAL, not here — very clean, well-tuned, high-partial-count choral takes should route there.
The single-voice case is VOICES-SINGING: narrow image, one f0 track.

explanation: football. For mixed ambience used AMBIENCE-SPORT. A sports chant.

discriminators: A stadium crowd is a mixture — walla bed, chants, applause swells, whistles, horns — so no single morphology owns it, and nothing in the spectrum says 'baseball'.
UCS reserves the mixed-with-game-sounds version for AMBIENCE-SPORT; this is the clean crowd. The chant component gives it more onset periodicity than CROWDS-WALLA, but the label is semantic.
Cheer swells pull it toward CROWDS-CHEERING and clap sections toward CROWDS-APPLAUSE; only text (venue, sport) resolves it.

explanation: with non-discernible words. See also various AMBIENCE categories.

discriminators: UCS: 'very clean crowd walla with non-discernible words'. Enough overlapping talkers that the individual syllabic trains average out — so it stays clearly VOICED (mid-band, formant-rich) while its 3–8 Hz syllabic modulation is SMEARED and the energy envelope goes flat (stationarity very high, crest low).
That is the exact inverse of CROWDS-CONVERSATION, where few talkers keep the syllabic peaks sharp and the envelope eventful.
Against CROWDS-QUIET the difference is level and brightness only — both are stationary voiced beds. Honest: narrowable.
Voiced, so it never competes with CROWDS-APPLAUSE.
